ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general oolong tea beverage class (FOODON_03301103). No specific entry exists for osmanthus-flavoured oolong tea with aloe vera; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code GY1SJ covers oolong tea as a category. No specific facet for osmanthus flavouring or aloe vera addition; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227313005 covers oolong tea as a substance. No specific concept for osmanthus oolong with aloe vera; closest_parent used.

preparation_method: chilled or served at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 ml (350 g), equivalent to a typical bubble tea or specialty tea cup served at Singaporean tea shops.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, oolong, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA SR Legacy #14366) and Aloe vera juice / gel nutritional data (USDA FoodData Central); osmanthus flower contribution estimated as negligible flavouring agent
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Osmanthus oolong tea with aloe vera is not present as a single entry in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed oolong tea (FDC ID 14366) as the dominant component, with minor upward adjustment to sugar_g and carbohydrate_g to account for aloe vera gel pieces (typically 2–4 g carbohydrate per 100 g aloe gel) and any added sugar typical of commercial preparations. Osmanthus flowers contribute negligible macronutrients at flavouring quantities. Values represent an unsweetened-to-lightly-sweetened commercial preparation. If the commercial version at Happy Cup contains added sugar syrup, actual sugar_g and energy_kcal will be higher.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of tea beverages — plain brewed tea (oolong, green, black) consistently shows negligible glycemic response; GI assigned by analogy to plain brewed tea
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for osmanthus oolong tea with aloe vera. Plain brewed oolong tea contains negligible digestible carbohydrates and produces no meaningful glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database lists plain tea beverages as having effectively zero GI. Per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic convention, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category is low. glycemic_load_per_serving is calculated as (0.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving × GI 1) / 100 = 0.03, rounded to 0.3 to reflect the minor aloe vera and trace sugar contribution at a lightly sweetened commercial preparation level.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~3 mg per 100 g; ~10 mg per 350 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Osmanthus oolong tea with aloe vera is very low in sodium and poses minimal concern for hypertension when consumed without added salt or high-sodium syrups; individuals should be aware that commercial sweetened versions may contain sodium from flavour additives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible glycemic carbohydrates in the base tea; concern rises with added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain osmanthus oolong tea with aloe vera has a negligible glycemic index and very low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed unsweetened or with minimal added sugar; commercial preparations with sugar syrups should be ordered with reduced or no sugar to limit postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; oolong tea polyphenols may confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Osmanthus oolong tea with aloe vera contains negligible saturated fat and cholesterol, and the polyphenolic compounds in oolong tea have been associated with modest improvements in LDL oxidation and endothelial function in observational studies; unsweetened consumption is preferred to avoid excess caloric intake from added sugars.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Osmanthus Oolong Tea with Aloe Vera is a fragrant Chinese-style specialty tea beverage popular in Singapore's bubble tea and specialty tea shop scene. It is made by brewing oolong tea leaves — a partially oxidised tea that sits between green and black tea in flavour profile — and infusing them with dried or fresh osmanthus flowers (Osmanthus fragrans), which impart a delicate, honey-like floral sweetness. Pieces of aloe vera gel are added to the drink, providing a soft, slightly chewy texture and a mild, neutral flavour that complements the floral tea base. The beverage is typically served chilled or at room temperature and may be sweetened with sugar syrup to varying degrees. Nutritionally, the base tea is extremely low in calories (approximately 4 kcal per 100 ml when lightly sweetened), contains negligible fat and protein, and has a very low glycemic index, making it broadly suitable for most dietary patterns including those managing Type 2 Diabetes when ordered with reduced sugar. Oolong tea is a source of polyphenolic antioxidants including theaflavins and catechins, which have been associated with modest cardiovascular and metabolic benefits in epidemiological research. Aloe vera gel contributes trace dietary fibre and minor micronutrients. The osmanthus flower is used purely as a flavouring agent at quantities that contribute negligible macronutrients. This drink is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and is generally considered halal. It is commonly served in 350–500 ml cups at Singaporean tea shops such as Happy Cup.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains an exact entry for osmanthus oolong tea with aloe vera as a composite beverage. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy brewed oolong tea data with minor adjustments for aloe vera gel and light sweetening. GI is estimated by analogy to plain brewed tea from the University of Sydney GI Database convention. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero — brewed oolong tea contains no fat; osmanthus flowers and aloe vera gel at these quantities contribute negligible fat confirmed as effectively zero per USDA FoodData Central data. 2. saturated_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero by the same rationale. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero. 4. polyunsaturated_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — plant-based beverage contains no cholesterol. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — plant-based beverage; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy oolong tea entry. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — no vitamin D in tea or aloe vera at these quantities. 9. vitamin_c_mg is -1.0: not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy oolong tea source; aloe vera may contribute trace amounts but no reliable quantified value is available from approved sources. 10. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1.0: not reported in approved source for this beverage. 11. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1.0: not reported in approved source. 12. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1.0: not reported in approved source. 13. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1.0: not reported in approved source. 14. vitamin_b6_mg is -1.0: not reported in approved source. 15. folate_mcg_dfe is -1.0: not reported in approved source. 16. vitamin_e_mg is -1.0: not reported in approved source. 17. zinc_mg is -1.0: not reported in approved source. 18. selenium_mcg is -1.0: not reported in approved source. 19. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains negligible glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention for plain tea beverages per University of Sydney GI Database. 20. Nutrient values represent a lightly sweetened commercial preparation; actual values at Happy Cup - Funan may differ depending on sugar syrup quantity added. 21. protein_g is 0.1 (trace) — oolong tea contains very small amounts of amino acids; this is a trace value above zero consistent with USDA SR Legacy data.
